data.title: Down Over 30% This Year: Analysts Double Down on These 3 Beaten-Down Stocks
data.description:
What should you do with a stock that’s been falling? Some would say, sell it now and cut your losses – but others say, look for opportunities. There are a myriad of reasons why a stock can fall, but Wall Street’s analysts will tell you that you need to read those reasons with care.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

In some cases, the decline reflects genuine deterioration – weakening fundamentals, shrinking demand, or a business model that no longer fits the market. Those are red flags investors need to recognize. In other instances, however, the pressure stems from temporary setbacks: a softer product cycle, tougher competition, or broader market volatility that sweeps up even solid companies.

The real challenge is distinguishing between structural problems and short-term turbulence. No one can consistently time the exact bottom, but identifying quality names that have been repriced can tilt the odds in your favor.

With that in mind, we turned to the TipRanks database to screen for stocks that have dropped sharply yet still get the backing of Wall Street analysts. Each of the three names below is down at least 30% so far in 2026, yet the analyst community remains constructive. Let’s take a closer look at why the analysts are doubling down.

Unity Software (U)

First on our list here is Unity Software, founded in 2004 in Denmark but currently based in San Francisco. Unity offers its users a software platform for creating video games – and not just to create them, but also to expand them, to gather an audience, to create an interactive experience, and to make it available across all major mobile, PC, console, and XR platforms. In short, Unity plays a foundational role in a major segment of the computing industry.

A short trip through Unity’s website shows that the company can offer a wide range of services, including 2D and 3D game creation, user acquisition apps so that creators can bring in an audience, and monetization apps that allow creators to generate revenue from their work. In addition, the Unity platform has applications in a wide range of non-gaming industries, including immersive training, retail, manufacturing, customer experience, and 3D training. Unity’s systems are compatible with both augmented and virtual reality devices, through designer and user interfaces.

Unity’s own users have access to a range of interactive connections, including livestreams and discussions, to get the most out of the platform by finding out what other users are doing with it. It’s part of Unity’s overall commitment to providing transparency in the service.

In its near-quarter century of operation, Unity has carved out a clear niche and built itself into an ~$8 billion company. In the second half of last year, Unity started to see its stock rise – but that trend reversed itself sharply in the last few weeks. The stock is now down 58% year to date.

The company is facing headwinds from the AI industry, where mega-cap firms like Google are taking the lead in AI and interactive world generation. In a more recent headwind, Unity’s revenue outlook for 1Q26, reported last week, came in with a range of $480 million to $490 million, well below the $494 million expected on Wall Street.

The soft outlook overshadowed a solid report. In 4Q25, Unity’s top line came to $503 million, up 10% year over year and beating the forecast by $10.18 million. The company’s bottom line, of 24 cents per share by non-GAAP measures, was 3 cents per share better than expected.

Oppenheimer analyst Martin Yang, in his recent note on Unity, explains why the stock remains a sound opportunity despite the headwinds. He says, “The recent sell-off, driven by fears that ‘world models’ like Google’s Project Genie will displace game engines, is fundamentally misplaced and ignores the distinct architectural role Unity plays in development. We view the current valuation as disconnected from the company’s improving fundamentals, specifically the successful re-acceleration of the Grow segment via the Vector, and disciplined cost management. We view the current risk/reward profile as favorable. We believe U will see accelerating revenue growth and margin expansion in 2026, validating its role not as a victim of AI, but as a primary platform for its deployment.”

Putting this into quantifiable terms, Yang rates the stock as Outperform (i.e., Buy), with a $32 price target that implies an upside of 72.5% on the one-year horizon. (To watch Yang’s track record, click here)

The 20 analyst reviews on file for Unity include 15 to Buy, 4 to Hold, and 1 to Sell, giving the stock its Moderate Buy consensus rating. U shares are trading for $18.55, and the $37.53 average target price indicates potential for a robust 102% one-year upside. (See  Unity Software stock forecast)

data.title: Morgan Stanley Says These 2 Momentum Stocks Could Climb Even Higher
data.description:
Investors are often drawn to stocks that are already outperforming. Even though the familiar caveat reminds us that historical returns don’t ensure future gains, sustained rallies tend to attract capital.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That dynamic underpins momentum investing. The strategy can be effective when price strength reflects solid fundamentals – companies operating in attractive markets, generating healthy profits, and executing against durable business models. In such cases, upward trends are not just technical patterns but signals of underlying business momentum.

Conviction tends to build further when leading Wall Street firms share that positive view. A review of the TipRanks database highlights two momentum stocks that have earned support from Morgan Stanley. The bank’s analysts see compelling reasons for further upside, making these names worth a closer examination.

TeraWulf (WULF)

The first stock we’ll look at, WULF, represents a company that lives on the leading edge of today’s tech world – a combination of data centers, high performance computing, and bitcoin mining. TereWulf has more than 30 years of experience drawing value from energy, and has five utility-scale data center facilities in operation. Two of these were built on the sites of coal-fired power plants, one on a repurposed industrial site; those locations bring advantages, allowing the company to make use of preexisting energy infrastructure without causing environmental damage.

TeraWulf’s five data campus locations have an aggregate total of nearly 2.8 gigawatts of infrastructure capacity. The company’s Lake Mariner facility, the 750 megawatt campus in Barker, New York, is also the site of TeraWulf’s bitcoin mining operations. Approximately 90% of the power drawn by this facility comes from zero-carbon sources.

Outside of bitcoin mining, TeraWulf makes its data centers available for contract operations, giving customers access to high-end, AI-capable computing power based on low-cost and sustainable energy production, and supported by redundant fiber access and networking, and built in full compliance with energy, physical, and regulatory security standards.

The AI boom of recent years has put a premium on quality data centers, and TeraWulf has been a direct beneficiary of that. The company’s stock has gained an impressive 243% over the past 12 months, the uptick coming against a backdrop of rising revenues.

Covering this stock for Morgan Stanley, analyst Stephen Byrd notes that the company provides a sound model for investors to gain access to the high-demand data center sector.

“For investors seeking maximum exposure to DC growth, and who share our thesis that there is a large excess of demand for compute relative to supply, WULF is an attractive way to achieve such exposure,” Byrd opined.

“TeraWulf has a strong track record of signing agreements with data center customers, and the management team has extensive experience in building a wide range of power infrastructure assets…. We project that the company will achieve success in converting the sites that currently do not have a Bitcoin-to-DC contract, at a present value that equates to ~$8/ watt (or an un-discounted value of $10/watt). We also assume that management achieves a 50% success rate in achieving the lower end of its targeted annual DC project growth of 250 MW/year in 2028-32,” the analyst added.

Byrd follows up these comments by rating WULF as Overweight (i.e., Buy) with a $37 price target that implies a robust 129% gain in the coming months. (To watch Byrd’s track record, click here)

Overall, the Street gives this stock a unanimous Strong Buy consensus rating, based on 6 recent positive reviews. The stock is priced at $16.18 and its $24.20 average price target indicates room for a 49.5% gain by this time next year. (See WULF stock forecast)

data.title: Goldman Sachs Names 3 ‘Strong Buy’ Energy Stocks with Room to Run
data.description:
A mix of factors has lifted oil prices after they hit a 12-month low in December. Heightened geopolitical tensions in the Middle East – particularly concerns about supply risks linked to Iran – have supported crude benchmarks, as well as ongoing uncertainty around production from sanctioned exporters such as Venezuela. Broader shifts in market sentiment tied to U.S. policy rhetoric and risk appetite have also contributed to volatility in energy markets.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Any sustained rise in oil prices would ripple through the broader economy. However, senior energy analysts at Goldman Sachs argue that volatility can also create opportunity. In a recent note, the team highlighted three energy stocks they believe are well positioned in the current environment.

Goldman’s selections span multiple segments of the energy complex, including exploration and production, midstream infrastructure, and utilities, reinforcing the case for diversification.

Each stock also carries a Strong Buy consensus rating on Wall Street, according to TipRanks data. With that context in mind, let’s take a closer look at what sets these names apart.

Viper Energy (VNOM)

Goldman’s exploration and production (E&P) pick is Viper energy, a limited partnership firm that owns and exploits oil and natural gas assets in North America. The company acquires assets in the form of mineral rights and royalty interests, located in high-margin hydrocarbon areas that are mainly undeveloped. Viper’s business model is designed to sustain free cash flow without requiring high demands on capital.

Viper is a subsidiary of the Texas-based E&P firm Diamondback Energy, and uses that relationship to inform its purchases of assets, particularly royalty interests. Diamondback, in turn, is the primary operator on Viper’s assets, and has a clear incentive to develop those acres for improved consolidated returns. The mutual relationship benefits both companies – and Viper’s shareholders.

The shareholders gain because they are the ‘primary business’ for Viper. The company has prioritized providing attractive shareholder returns as a business goal, which it does by loading up on mineral and royalty assets in the Permian basin, one of Texas’ richest hydrocarbon-bearing formations.

Investors should be aware of a couple of important developments with Viper in recent months. In August of last year, the company acquired Sitio Royalties in an all-equity transaction that brought Sitio’s assets under Viper’s rubric. The new assets were integrated into Viper’s operations during 3Q25.

This was followed in November by the announcement that Viper had entered into an agreement to sell off its non-Permian assets to an affiliate of GRP Energy Capital and Warwick Capital Partners. This transaction was valued at $670 million, and is expected to close during 1Q26.

Viper pays out a 33-cent regular dividend to shareholders, with the last payment sent out on November 20. It was accompanied by a 25-cent variable dividend – Viper has a history in recent years of adding variable dividends to the regular payment. The regular dividend annualizes to $1.32 per common share and gives a 3% forward yield, while the addition of the variable increased the yield to 5.2%.

In 3Q25, the last period reported, Viper realized an adjusted net income of $0.40 per share, beating the forecast by a penny. The company finished Q3 with $53 million in cash and liquid assets on hand.

For Goldman’s Neil Mehta, an expert on the energy industry, Viper presents a combination of sound valuation and a positive outlook going forward. He writes of the company, “We see VNOM as a stock with positive valuation support given shares trade at a 10% FCF yield on our 2027 estimates (using $70/bbl Brent oil) – a modest premium relative to oily E&P peer average of 11%. We continue to highlight the benefits of VNOM’s no-capex royalty business model which supports VNOM’s meaningful return of capital commitments (at least 75% of cash available for distribution to shareholders) and low dividend breakeven of ~$30/bbl WTI. While the announced non-Permian assets sales are still pending close, we remain constructive on the long-term benefits stemming from the close relationship between VNOM and FANG as well as the shareholder return profile of the pro-forma, pure-play Permian business in an uncertain oil environment.”

Mehta, a 5-star analyst, puts a Buy rating on VNOM along with a $54 price target that points toward a one-year upside potential of 22%. (To watch Mehta’s track record, click here)

The Strong Buy consensus rating on VNOM shares is unanimous, based on 11 recent positive analyst reviews. The stock is trading for $44.25 and its $50.8 average target price implies a gain of 15% in the coming year. (See VNOM stock forecast)

data.title: Down But Not Out: Piper Sandler Names Its Top 2 Software Stocks for 2026
data.description:
The rapid expansion of AI in recent years has created a more complicated backdrop for the software industry. While AI continues to drive productivity gains and new revenue opportunities across technology, it is also introducing structural pressures.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

By improving efficiencies, AI systems are reducing headcounts – and that has implications for software companies, who sell subscriptions and products on a per-seat basis. The situation is also complicated on the coding side, where AI makes it possible for customers to code their own apps, rather than buying purpose-built software.

In a recent note on the software sector, Piper Sandler analyst Billy Fitzsimmons outlined the firm’s current take on this beaten-down segment.

“Despite broad-based pullbacks after negative reactions to the initial C4Q prints, we still we believe select pockets of software are better insulated from AI disruption. AI demand is outpacing supply and AI investments are increasing compute/ storage requirements, benefitting the hyperscalers and consumption growth. Meanwhile, vertical software names possess specialized workflows, proprietary data, and strong customer relationships. The customers for specific verticals (the trades, construction) were later SaaS adopters, so it is hard to see them as early vibe coding trailblazers,” the analyst opined.

With that framework in mind, Fitzsimmons highlights two software stocks he believes can buck the broader trend and post gains in 2026, calling both his top picks. One is a well-known name, while the other flies more under the radar – but both carry Buy ratings from Piper Sandler, and as a check of the TipRanks database shows – from Wall Street overall.

Microsoft (MSFT)

We’ll start with a look at Microsoft, the world’s largest software firm – and one of the largest tech companies in the world. Microsoft’s $3.07 trillion market cap ranks it as the fourth-largest publicly traded firm on Wall Street. While Microsoft originally built itself up on the success of its software products – Windows and Office are still the gold standard in PC software – it’s been finding strong success recently in the related fields of AI and cloud computing.

On the AI side, we should note that Microsoft was an early backer of the technology, and put its money where its mouth was. Microsoft started funding OpenAI, the creator of ChatGPT, with a $1 billion investment in 2019. In the years since, the software giant has backed OpenAI to the tune of over $13 billion – and last year, the two companies formalized their relationship for the future. Microsoft now owns a 27% stake in the AI firm, and OpenAI has committed to spending $250 billion over the next several years on Microsoft’s Azure cloud computing platform. The overall agreement, for Microsoft, represents a massive return on its original investment.

It also underscores the growing importance of Azure to Microsoft. The subscription-based cloud platform has become one of Microsoft’s hottest products, and the company’s Intelligent Cloud segment, which includes Azure, now accounts for just over 40% of total revenues.

Microsoft is responding to this growth by investing heavily in AI and cloud data centers, which brings us to a sore point for investors and analysts. In the past year, the company has implemented a 65% jump in capital expenditure on data centers, to a total of $37.5 billion. That’s a massive outlay, by any standards, and while the company’s goal of doubling its data center footprint is laudable, the sheer scale of the spending has prompted worries that it may not be sustainable.

Turning to the company’s last quarterly report, which covered fiscal 2Q26, we find that the top line, of $81.3 billion, was up 17% year-over-year and beat expectations by $993.7 million. Microsoft realized non-GAAP earnings of $4.14 per share, for a 24% year-over-year gain – and 22 cents per share better than the forecast. Intelligent Cloud revenue was listed as $32.9 billion, up 29% year-over-year.

We should note that, despite the strong figures, Microsoft’s stock has slipped by 16% since the earnings report. As noted, worries about the size and expense of the data center expansion along with Azure growth that slightly missed expectations lie behind that dip.

The recent softness in Microsoft’s stock is a flashing neon sign for Piper Sandler, saying ‘opportunity.’ Analyst Fitzsimmons writes of the company, “We would remain buyers on any MSFT weakness given 1) AI demand continues to exceed supply (the Azure growth rate as largely handicapped by capacity not slowing demand) and 2) total capex growth is expected to accelerate y/y in FY26 with a pivot towards short-lived assets (GPUs/ CPUs) to support immediate capacity constraints. MSFT is certainly not an out of consensus pick, but in an environment where there are existential questions around the relevance of software, we think it makes sense to stick to a name like MSFT with one of the strongest AI stories.”

Fitzsimmons puts an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating on Microsoft, along with a $600 price target that suggests a one-year upside potential of 48% for the stock. (To watch Fitzsimmons’ track record, click here)

There are 36 recent analyst reviews on file for Microsoft, and the 32 to 4 split, favoring Buy over Hold, gives the stock its Strong Buy consensus rating. The shares are priced at $404.37 and the average target price, of $593.38, indicates room for 47% growth in the coming year. (See MSFT stock forecast)

data.title: MU and SNDK: Analysts Pound the Table on These 2 Top Semiconductor Stocks
data.description:
The memory chip market is going through a massive boom phase, thanks in large part to the skyrocketing demand from AI, which needs huge amounts of fast, reliable memory to run complex models. HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) DRAM is in high demand for its ultra-fast speeds in GPUs and AI accelerators, while NAND flash provides large, cost-effective storage for all that data.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The appetite for these advanced memories is so strong that it’s outpacing supply, leading to widespread shortages and making it a seller’s market. As a result, companies and cloud providers are racing to secure the memory they need to keep up with increasingly sophisticated AI workloads.

Semiconductor memory specialists such as Micron (NASDAQ:MU) and SanDisk (NASDAQ:SNDK) have been reaping the rewards, with shares of both piling on the gains over the past year as sales have gone through the roof. However, given AI’s insatiable need for memory, Street analysts see more good times ahead for these names. So, let’s see what still makes these two memory stalwarts a unique proposition in 2026.

Micron

First up, memory giant Micron, a company heavily associated with the boom-and-bust dynamics of the memory industry. It’s a simple cycle really: when demand surges – often from consumer electronics like smartphones, PCs, or gaming consoles – prices spike and manufacturers ramp up production. But once supply catches up or demand slows, prices can collapse just as quickly, leaving producers with excess inventory and shrinking profits. These cycles have made memory one of the most volatile sectors in tech, with fortunes rising and falling in tandem with short-term shifts in supply and demand.

You could argue that with Micron shares up by 300% over the past year, it has clearly enjoyed an up cycle, but the thing is, this cycle is different from others. Because, unlike past waves, the current memory boom is powered by AI infrastructure, and AI workloads require massive, ongoing memory capacity for data centers, GPUs, and accelerators.

In fact, Micron has said that its all its HBM is essentially fully booked through 2026, as intense AI data center demand has absorbed available supply. Meanwhile, the demand/supply imbalance has also resulted in massive price increases.

These developments were on display in the company’s fiscal first quarter (November quarter) readout. Revenue reached $13.64 billion, topping estimates by $760 million, while adj. EPS reached $4.78, exceeding expectations by $0.82. The FQ2 guide was even more impressive. Micron guided for F2Q revenue of $18.7 billion at the midpoint and adj. EPS of $8.42 at the midpoint. The Street was only expecting $14.23 billion and $4.49, respectively.

According to UBS’s Timothy Arcuri, an analyst ranked in 4th spot among the thousands of Street stock experts, all signs point to further success for Micron. “In conjunction with our latest round of industry checks, we once again flag strengthening pricing dynamics across both core DRAM and NAND,” the 5-star analyst said. “We are hearing more investors questioning the sustainability of the upcycle, but we think things will last long enough and MU EPS will go high enough that it is hard to see how the stock does not keep working. We move EPS estimates higher yet again and now see EPS of ~$54 in C2026 and ~$75 in C2027E – meaning that MU trades at only ~5x earnings while HDD stocks trade at >2x this multiple assuming a very rosy 2-yr scenario for HDDs. We still don’t get why this should be the case…”

Conveying his confidence, Arcuri rates the shares a Buy, while his $450 price target offers 12-month upside of 21%. (To watch Arcuri’s track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, MU stock gets an additional 26 Buys and 1 Hold, for a Strong Buy consensus rating. The forecast currently calls for one-year returns of 5%, given the average target clocks in at $391.59. (See Micron stock forecast)

data.title: Seeking 2 High-Income Dividend Stocks? Analysts Suggest 2 Names Yielding at Least 9%
data.description:
So far in 2026, markets have been anything but linear, with periods of swings that have kept investors on edge. Major U.S. indexes experienced a downturn around mid-January when tariff fears and sell-offs among big tech names pushed key benchmarks lower, only for stocks to rally back over the past few trading sessions as buyers returned.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

There are still potential headwinds building. The Supreme Court has yet to rule on President Trump’s tariff policy, but is expected to do so in the near future; inflationary pressures remain unsettled; and in Washington’s hyper-partisan environment, there is always the risk of additional government shutdowns. Any or all of these could hit, and push the markets toward correction territory.

This leads us to U.S. Bank’s recent advice: “In this environment, investors often focus less on predicting the next downdraft and more on building staying power through different market regimes. The core message remains straightforward: stay invested and diversified despite volatility and uncertainty, because significant market swings are not new.”

One key place to diversify your portfolio: the high-income dividend stocks. While usually seen as defensive plays – and definitely filling that role here – these shares keep their key advantage even when times are good and the bulls are running. That is, they will always provide a steady income stream.

At their best, div stocks will add high yields to it, at least 9% in some cases. Using the TipRanks database, we’ve pulled up the details on two such dividend stocks that are also getting the ‘thumbs up’ from Wall Street’s analysts. Here’s a closer look.

Trinity Capital (TRIN)

Arizona-based Trinity Capital, the first stock on our list, is a BDC, a business development company, one of the many such investment funds that exist to provide capital infusions to the small- and mid-sized business sector that drives so much of the US economy. Trinity gives its own clients access to credit, and then turns around and gives its shareholders returns that are both stable and consistent.

Trinity does this by carefully vetting its own clientele. The BDC invests its capital in multiple fields, including tech, equipment, life sciences, sponsor finance, and even asset-based lending. As of September 30 last year, Trinity has originated $5.1 billion in total fundings since its 2008 founding, in a total of 449 investment targets. The company currently has $2.6 billion in assets under management.

BDCs get a tax advantage if they return 90% of their taxable income to investors, and Trinity, since its 2021 IPO, has paid out $411 million in cumulative distributions to shareholders. A large part of this was distributed through 23 consecutive quarters of consistent dividend payments.

The most recent dividend payment was declared on December 17 for the fourth quarter of 2025 at a rate of 51 cents per share. This same declaration also marked the company’s transition from a quarterly to a monthly payout of the dividend, meaning this payment will be delivered in three installments – January, February, and March – each of 17 cents per share. At the 51-cent quarterly rate, the dividend annualizes to $2.04 per share and gives a forward yield of 12.6%.

This past November, Trinity released its quarterly results for 3Q25. The company’s total investment income came to $75.6 million, a total that was up 22% year-over-year and beat the forecast by $1.17 million. On the bottom line, the net investment income (NII) of $37 million came to 52 cents per share – and missed the estimates by a penny. Despite that bottom-line miss, however, the NII is sufficient to fully cover the company’s quarterly dividend rate.

For Clear Street analyst Mickey Schleien, this BDC presents a solid investment choice. He writes of the company, “We believe Trinity Capital offers an attractive risk-adjusted income opportunity among internally managed BDCs, combining robust portfolio yields, good dividend coverage, and emerging fee-based optionality, while trading at an attractive valuation relative to its peers. In our view, TRIN’s internally managed structure, portfolio investment interest rate floors, disciplined underwriting, and differentiated access to venture-backed borrowers support sustainable earnings while trading at a discount to peer averages.”

Getting down to basics, he explains why the risk/reward is favorable, adding, “Although TRIN’s borrower profile and second-lien exposure imply somewhat greater credit risk than many traditional middle-market lenders, we believe this risk is to an extent mitigated by relatively low balance leverage, sponsor support, diversification, and active portfolio management.”

In Schleien’s view, all of this adds up to a Buy rating, and his $18 price target implies a one-year upside potential of 11%. Add in the dividend yield, and the total one-year return on this stock can hit 23.6%. (To watch Schleien’s track record, click here)

The 5 recent analyst reviews here split to 4 Buys and 1 Sell, for a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The stock is selling for $16.16, and its $16.90 average target price indicates a modest gain of 4.5% for the year ahead. (See TRIN stock forecast)

data.title: PLTR and SNOW: Daniel Ives Names His Top AI Stocks to Buy Amid a “Software Garage Sale”
data.description:
The start of the new year did not mark a reset for software stocks – instead, it continued the sell-off that began last year. Investors have grown more cautious as concerns build that the rapid spread of AI, especially generative and agentic technologies, could increasingly pressure the market share and pricing power of established software companies.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

This worry is particularly acute in regard to the seat-based models that have proven so successful with SaaS subscription providers. New AI tools coming to the market both promise and threaten – depending on your point of view – to automate more of the high-value, time-consuming work, in marketing, sales, and legal, that these legacy subscription systems have been handling.

While legacy software providers have, in general, provided safe and stable revenue streams for investors until now, that predictable model is threatening to break down under the impact of automated AI agents. With the stability of the software industry at risk, more and more analysts are turning pessimistic about the sector.

Wedbush’s Daniel Ives, who is rated among the top 4% of Wall Street’s analysts by TipRanks, is taking a more measured approach. While acknowledging the risks that software is facing, he believes that the industry bears might be running too far.

“We believe the market is baking in a doomsday scenario for software companies in the near-term, which we believe is extremely overblown, as many customers won’t be willing to put their data at risk to capitalize on AI implementation strategies until there is less risk with these migration projects. Is AI a headwind in the near-term for software? YES!…however, the magnitude of this software sell-off is a major head scratcher and is factoring in an Armageddon scenario for the sector that is far from reality in our view,” Ives opined.

Ives doesn’t pull his punches, and based on that stance, he is recommending that investors buy into two big names in data analysis software: Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) and Snowflake (NASDAQ:SNOW). Both are heavily involved in AI, and Ives is calling both as ‘Buys’ in his recent software industry report. Let’s give them a closer look, and find out why this 5-star analyst is so bullish.

Palantir

Palantir, the first of Ives’ picks that we’re looking at, is a tech company that has been in operation since 2003, focusing its efforts on data management and analytics, and artificial intelligence. Palantir was an early developer and user of AI, its data analytic platforms and tools have long been known for integrating AI into the mix.

The company’s flagship product is the AIP, or AI Platform, a data analysis platform designed from the get-go to make use of AI at all levels, including both the hard data management and the user interface side. For the user, the AIP is an intuitive tool, and the AI and language models allow users to interact with the system in natural language and in their native tongues. As a practical matter, this means that users don’t need any special computer training to use Palantir’s AIP—queries and instructions can be entered in plain text, and responses can be read the same way. The whole system is designed to emphasize the strengths of human users and their intuition in combination with the raw processing power of AI.

Palantir’s AI-powered data systems have been popular with large-scale commercial enterprises and governmental agencies, and a look at the company’s news releases announcing new partnerships and contracts shows this clearly. In recent times, since mid-December, Palantir has announced partnerships and/or collaborations with the French domestic intelligence agency DGSI; the multinational insurance group AIG; and with the UK’s Sovereign AI, the AI infrastructure and solutions provider. These announcements simply underline Palantir’s solid position as a major global provider of AI-powered data solutions.

In Palantir’s last financial results, released on February 2 and covering both Q4 and full-year 2025, the company showed a set of strong gains. For the full year, the company’s US revenue was up 75% y/y and reached $3.32 billion. In 2025, US commercial revenue was up 109% and US government revenue was up 55%. Overall, Palantir’s total revenue in 2025 came to $4.475 billion, up 56% from 2024. The company reported an adjusted free cash flow for the year of $2.270 billion.

Zooming into Q4, we see that Palantir’s total quarterly revenue of $1.407 billion was up 70% from 4Q24 and beat the forecast by $65.4 million. Palantir’s bottom line, the adjusted EPS of 25 cents, beat expectations by 2 cents per share. US commercial revenue in the quarter grew by an impressive 137%. Palantir continued its habit of securing strong contracts, and in the final quarter of 2025 it closed 180 deals worth at least $1 million.

We should note, however, that shares in PLTR are down 27% for the year-to-date. The share price decline is related to investor worries about the company’s high valuation, and its ability to continue accelerating along its growth track.

For Ives, however, those aren’t worries. He sees Palantir as the natural leader in the AI pack, and a company capable of maintaining its momentum. As enterprises move from AI experimentation to production, Palantir’s value proposition becomes more relevant, not less as AI demand is leading to new uses where speed-to-deployment and outcome-driven ROI matter more than model performance.”

data.title: SanDisk and Seagate: Analysts Bang the Drum on the Hottest Tech Stocks Today
data.description:
There’s a full month of 2026 behind us now, so it’s getting time to start talking about trends for the year. In the tech sector, one thing is clear: worries about an AI bubble are persisting, and it’s putting pressure on the industry’s hyperscalers.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Those giants – think Microsoft and Nvidia here – are feeling the headwinds of high valuations and industry saturation. But at the same time, demand for memory, particularly high-end data storage, is booming.

AI simply can’t function without a solid data-storage backing, since the models require a massive database both for training and everyday operation, and that database in turn demands huge investment in memory storage. The result was predictable, with demand for memory running far ahead and pushing prices higher, along with profit margins for memory suppliers.

That’s why Wall Street is banging the drum for SanDisk (NASDAQ:SNDK) and Seagate (NASDAQ:STX). Positioned at the heart of AI-driven storage demand, both stocks have skyrocketed in recent months, putting them among the market’s hottest tech plays. Let’s give them a closer look.

SanDisk (SNDK)

We’ll start with SanDisk, which is probably best known for its ubiquitous USB thumb drives. These convenient little data storage units make it easy to keep vital files and information portable – and SanDisk is a leader in this market. The company was founded back in 1988, and was publicly traded from 1995 to 2016, when it was acquired by Western Digital. Early last year, Western Digital spun SanDisk off as a private entity, and now trades on the NASDAQ. Since its spin-off and return to the public markets, SanDisk has seen its stock skyrocket – the shares are up ~1,800% since SNDK began trading, and have posted a 193% gain just this year. The company currently boasts a $98 billion market cap.

In addition to USB drives, SanDisk is also a major supplier of solid state drives (SSDs) for both external and internal use, as well as embedded form factors. The company’s data storage solutions are based on NAND flash technology, and are strongly favored in edge devices and in data center applications. SanDisk has a global footprint, with more than 10,000 employees working in over 30 countries. To protect its intellectual property, the key to its success, SanDisk has acquired over 11,000 patents worldwide.

Data center demand alone is proving to be a powerful driver for SanDisk’s business, and in fiscal 2Q26, which was reported on January 29, the company reported a 64% sequential gain in its data center business, and a year-over-year increase of 76%. The company’s data center segment generated revenue of $440 million in the fiscal second quarter.

SanDisk’s 2Q26 report was a blowout in other ways, too. The company’s total revenue, of $3.03 billion, was up 61% year-over-year, and beat the forecast by $337 million. At the bottom line, the company’s non-GAAP EPS, at $6.20, was up 404% from the prior-year period – and was $2.66 better than had been anticipated. SanDisk has $1.54 billion in cash and liquid assets at the end of the quarter.

For Raymond James analyst Melissa Fairbanks, all of this adds up to a new stock that is upwardly mobile and still going strong. She writes, “Even after the recent move – up more than 16x (sixteen times, not percent) since the spin-off from Western Digital a year ago – we see opportunities for further upside, not surprisingly driven by the demand and pricing implications of an unprecedented datacenter/AI cycle. We also arguably can’t accurately predict revenue or earnings against the backdrop of guidance for 57% upside in revenue versus current consensus, and an EPS beat of 2.5x expectations. That said, we know demand is exceptionally strong and likely only growing, supply is tightening to the point of potentially being sold out for years, and we see limited new supply coming online in the near term – supporting continued strong (an understatement) pricing going forward.”

Summing up her own bottom line on SNDK, the analyst adds, “Net, while we have favored the DRAM suppliers recently, we now see a sustainably more favorable environment for NAND as well, and as such view SNDK as a key beneficiary of a mix-shift toward longer-lived datacenter builds, prioritizing supply away from higher-velocity edge/consumer.”

Fairbanks puts an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating here, along with a $725 price target. Since then, however, shares have kept moving up and that figure now factors in just modest upside of 4%. (To watch Fairbanks’ track record, click here)

The 16 recent analyst reviews here break down to 12 Buys and 4 Holds, for a Strong Buy consensus rating. The stock’s recent rapid gains have pushed the $695.51 trading price 15% above the $589.38 average target price. With this in mind, watch out for price target hikes or rating downgrades shortly. data.title: Analysts Say ‘Load Up’ on These 2 Nuclear Power Stocks — Here’s Why
data.description:
The widespread push toward energy sector decarbonization is well underway, and usually turns investor attention toward wind or solar power. But nuclear power is rapidly gaining interest – it’s inherently more reliable than wind or solar, and leaves a minimal carbon footprint – although long-term storage of spent fuel does remain an issue.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That shift is being driven by rising demand for reliable baseload power – particularly from energy-hungry data centers – a trend Northland analyst Jeff Grampp is tracking closely.

“It seems every week there are announcements of new companies or governments expanding nuclear capacity or revisiting prior stances on nuclear as the world seeks out ways to expand, accelerate or incentivize new nuclear capacity. Whether that is DOE allocating billions to U.S. nuclear fuel supply or Meta striking deals with multiple nuclear reactor players, momentum is strong for accelerated nuclear adoption, in our view,” Grampp noted.

The expansion of the nuclear industry has also caused regulatory headaches, as the rules and regulations that govern how large users – such as data centers and power plants – connect to the grid. That may be easing, as this past December the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) directed PJM – the US electricity sector’s largest regional transmission organization – to revise its tariff structures and lay out clearer, standardized rules for large-scale grid connections, project approvals, cost allocations, and other regulatory bottlenecks.

With demand climbing and the prospect of regulatory relief improving the outlook, the setup for the nuclear industry is turning increasingly positive. Wall Street is already taking notice – and analysts are pointing investors toward nuclear power stocks. We used the TipRanks database to pinpoint two of the latest calls.

Constellation Energy (CEG)

The first nuclear power stock we’re looking at here, Constellation Energy, has been an independent energy operator since 1999, and today is well known as a power provider with generation assets in wind, solar, hydroelectric, and natural gas – as well as nuclear power plants. The company’s headquarters are in Baltimore, Maryland, and its power generation – capable of supplying some 32,400 megawatts – supports more than 20 million homes and businesses. Constellation has set a goal of eliminating all of its greenhouse gas emissions by 2040; to that end, it is already the nation’s largest operator of carbon-free nuclear power.

One important part of Constellation’s ongoing efforts to expand its nuclear power fleet operations is its involvement in the restart of the Crane Clean Energy Center. Many readers will likely recognize Crane by its older name, Three Mile Island – the site of the nation’s worst nuclear accident back in the 1970s. Renamed Crane, the long-dormant Unit 1 is scheduled to restart next year.

This past November, Constellation arranged a $1 billion loan deal with the Department of Energy to fund the restart operations. We should note that the company is working on this project as part of an agreement to provide electrical power to Microsoft, in support of the tech giant’s data center expansion projects.

In another major step that will interest data center investors worried about power supplies, Constellation in early January announced the completion of its acquisition agreement with Calpine Corporation, in a transaction valued at $16.4 billion; that figure included $4.5 billion in cash. Including Constellation’s assumption of Calpine’s debt, the entire transaction is valued at well over $26 billion.

The Calpine deal adds that company’s natural gas and geothermal power generation facilities to Constellation’s generation fleet. This makes Constellation the nation’s largest single electric producer and gives the company a solid conventional power foundation to rely on as it expands its nuclear generation capacity.

This stock has caught the attention of Wells Fargo analyst Shar Pourreza, who states up-front his bullish take: “CEG remains our Best IPP [Independent Power Producer] Idea—we see ideal mix and scale of assets, leadership position on nuclear contracting, with FERC/PJM debottlenecking regulatory uncertainty and creating opportunities for CEG. CEG goes above and beyond our absolute call on IPPs with no significant fundamental changes since our LOC (i.e. no data center slowdown, PJM colocation rules moving forward and overall supportive policy backdrop).”

Getting into details, Pourreza outlines several reasons why Constellation should show strength this year, including, “Near-term catalysts include: 1) hyperscaler deal by year-end, 2) Calpine accretion, 3) we see costs coming down on the Crane restart, and 4) uprate opportunities (~300MW for Crane) will likely move above 2 GW identified fleetwide. Overall the NT catalyst path is constructive. Additionally, we see the FERC/PJM order making a stronger thesis to move forward with CEG PJM nuclear portfolio contracting with optionality around both FOM and colocation alternatives (incl. partial colo).”

When he quantifies his stance, the 5-star analyst puts an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, along with a $460 price target that points toward a one-year upside potential of 70%. (To watch Pourreza’s track record, click here)

The 11 recent analyst reviews here split 8 to 3 in favor of Buy over Hold, for a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The shares are priced at $270.88, and the $407.36 average price target implies a gain of 50% this coming year. (See CEG stock forecast)

That kind of environment tends to reward preparation rather than prediction. One approach that often stands out in such periods is the passive dividend strategy.

Dividend stocks are typically associated with defense, but their appeal goes well beyond market downturns. Their defining feature is the steady income stream they provide, and that reliability becomes especially valuable when price action turns unpredictable. Whether markets are rising or falling, consistent cash payouts can help anchor returns and smooth out volatility.

With that in mind, let’s take a closer look at a pair of high-yield dividend stocks. These are shares offering yields of at least 8%, making them difficult to overlook in any market climate. Rithm Capital (RITM)

We’ll start with a REIT, a logical place to look for champion dividend stocks. Rithm was founded in 2013, and in the years since it has become a leading investment company in the field of mortgage servicing rights (MSRs). These instruments made up the bulk of Rithm’s early portfolio; today, the company pursues a more varied strategy, and holds a wide range of real estate assets. These assets include residential or mortgage loans, commercial real estate, single-family rentals, business purpose loans, and consumer loans, all of which contribute to Rithm’s mission of providing long-term shareholder value with robust returns.

Rithm currently has $47 billion in total assets on its books, of which some $8.7 billion is stockholder’s equity in Rithm Capital.

On this past December 1, Rithm completed its acquisition of Crestline Management. The financial details of the transaction were not disclosed publicly – but bringing Crestline fully under Rithm’s umbrella added $17 billion to the REIT’s total assets.

Turning to financial results, we find that in Rithm’s last reported quarter, 3Q25, the company’s bottom line, reported as a non-GAAP figure of earnings available for distribution per diluted common share, came to 54 cents.

That figure is important, because it fully covers Rithm’s dividend. The dividend payment was last declared on December 18 for January 30 payment, at a rate of $0.25 per common share. data.title: Goldman Sachs Picks 2 Stocks That Let Investors Buy the Dip or Ride the Momentum
data.description:
In stock trading, wins and losses hinge on countless moving parts, but choosing the right strategy can tip the odds in your favor. That sounds simple enough in theory, yet in practice, it’s anything but. To see why, consider two of the market’s most popular – and opposing – approaches: buying the dip and riding momentum.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Both do exactly what their names imply. Buying the dip means scooping up beaten-down stocks that still boast solid fundamentals. Riding momentum, on the other hand, is about jumping aboard stocks already on the rise and betting the rally still has fuel left in the tank. Either way, you’re swimming against the current – buying when others are selling, or wagering that “past performance doesn’t guarantee future results” won’t apply this time.

The appeal of these strategies lies in the logic behind them; strong companies can stumble temporarily, creating opportunity for patient investors. Meanwhile, sustained rallies can signal deeper strength and further upside ahead. The real challenge is knowing when each strategy is likely to work – and when it’s a trap.

That’s never an easy call, but the analysts at Goldman Sachs think they’ve spotted one stock that fits each playbook in today’s market. Using data from the TipRanks database, we took a closer look at both names. Here’s what Goldman sees – and why these two stocks stand out.

Spotify Technology (SPOT)

We’ll start with a stock on a dip, Spotify. This is the popular music-streaming company from Sweden that has, over the past 20 years, propelled itself into a leading position in the online music streaming business. Spotify is known for its subscription model, its large library of available music and media, and its large audience.

The company offers free users a basic level of access and function, while paying subscribers can dip into a library that includes over 100 million songs, 7 million podcasts, and a growing list of audiobooks. In its report for 3Q25, Spotify announced that it had reached a total of 713 million monthly active users (MAUs), a key metric for an online subscription service. This figure was up 11% year-over-year, and included 281 million premium subscribers. The premium subscriber figure was up 12% year-over-year.

The company was founded in 2006 and went public in 2018. In recent months, the shares have been sliding downward – SPOT has lost approximately 35% since peaking last June. A number of issues have caused investors to pull back, including Spotify’s listing of ‘AI artists’ on its platform and some controversy regarding royalty payments to copyright holders, while slower revenue growth hasn’t helped matters either.

We should note that Spotify has been raising its subscription prices, and earlier this month announced that, as of the coming February billing period, the new subscription price will rise from $11.99 per month to $12.99.

For Goldman analyst Eric Sheridan, the new subscription pricing and the increase in regular users are only two of the reasons to see strength in Spotify. He writes ,“Looking over a long-term horizon, we see SPOT as well positioned against several long-term operating themes (which are reflected in our above-Street estimates) in the coming years. Among those themes would be: 1) steady Premium subscription price increases (at a regular cadence of 1-2 years and staggered by geography); 2) the introduction of new premium pricing tiers; 3) healthy MAU growth (particularly in emerging markets) and steady conversion of ad-supported users into paid subscribers; & 4) reacceleration of advertising revenue growth in ‘26 & beyond, aided by both the launch/scaling of the Spotify Ad Exchange (SAX) and programmatic ad buying capabilities and monetization of video podcast ad inventory.”

Summing up, Sheridan adds, “In addition, we see a solid runway for gross profit dollar growth & margin improvement driven by: 1) modestly improving unit economics on core Music product including leverage on royalty payments; 2) leverage on ‘fixed’ expenses within cost of revenues, most notably Podcast expenses; & 3) scaling of high incremental gross margin revenue streams in Advertising.”

Taken all together, these comments back up Sheridan’s Buy rating on SPOT, while his $700 price target implies a one-year upside of 39%. (To watch Sheridan’s track record, click here)

Spotify has earned a Strong Buy consensus rating on the Street, based on 25 analyst reviews that include 19 Buys and 6 Holds. The stock is trading for $503.92 and its $717.7 average target price is actually more bullish than the Goldman view here, implying a gain of 42% in the next 12 months. (See SPOT stock forecast)

data.title: From Bitcoin to Balance Sheets: Analyst Names 3 Crypto Stocks to Buy — MSTR, COIN and HOOD
data.description:
It’s easy to think fintech has simplified finance, and in many ways, it has. Digital platforms make transactions feel faster and trading more convenient in an increasingly online world. But much of that speed is only on the surface. Banks still take days to settle many transactions, and the back-end systems have barely changed. What has changed is how customers interact with the financial system, not how the system itself operates.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Cryptocurrency breaks from this model because these assets were built for a digital environment from the start, using infrastructure designed for direct and rapid settlement. That distinction has drawn Wall Street’s attention, but regulatory uncertainty continues to weigh on the sector. In the US, investors are waiting for resolution on the CLARITY Act, which remains stalled in the Senate and could shape how crypto trading is regulated.

Against this backdrop, Cantor analyst Ramsey El-Assal has been taking a deep dive into the segment, and highlights its unique selling point. “From crypto’s inception, ‘decentralization’ has been a primary rallying cry of a libertarian-oriented community of early adopters. Removing fee-taking-intermediaries from financial transactions and ensuring that crypto remains ‘pristine collateral’ that cannot be debased by authorities are oft-cited reasons for the technology’s existence. Not only are these noble motivations, but they make a great deal of sense in the context of crypto being a store of value or an asset. The stunning success of Bitcoin—whose uncompromising design elevates decentralization at the expense of financial usefulness—is a case in point,” El-Assal wrote.

El-Assal goes on to lay out where opportunities in the industry are likely to open up: “We believe the primary value accrual in the crypto/blockchain ecosystem is likely to shift away from private projects and decentralized protocols and towards new and existing public companies that find ways to reap the benefits of blockchain technology, while also remaining compliant and generally insync with mainstream financial services. This thought applies primarily to fintech product innovators but certainly includes capital markets innovators such as MSTR and its emulators.”

These comments back up the Cantor analyst’s stance, as he names three crypto stocks to buy in today’s market. Let’s give them a closer look and find out just what makes them stand out. And with some assistance from the TipRanks database, we can also gauge general Street sentiment toward these names.

Strategy (MSTR)

The first crypto company we’ll look at here is Strategy. The company has been buying – and holding – bitcoin since 2020, and currently holds over 712,000 of the flagship crypto. At current trading prices, this asset base is valued at ~$62.5 billion – and makes Strategy the world’s largest bitcoin treasury company.

Strategy’s name embodies the company’s outlook on the business world: the firm accumulates bitcoins, and promotes the use of them as a digital capital asset. As a publicly traded company, Strategy offers its investors a controlled exposure to the crypto markets, and a chance to gain from bitcoin without buying bitcoin. In addition, Strategy also offers customers an AI-powered business intelligence/enterprise analytics platform that is trusted by such large names as Pfizer, KFC, Visa, and Sony.

That was how the company got its start, but the key for Strategy is in the bitcoin treasury. Recent disclosures show that the company purchased $264.1 million worth of bitcoin from January 20 to January 25, acquisitions that followed even higher purchases in the previous week; in the 7 days ending on January 19, the company purchased $2.13 billion worth of the crypto.

For El-Assal, in laying out the Cantor view, the most important factor is Strategy’s ability to maintain its bitcoin investments while offering investors access through varied types of exposure. He writes, “We believe MSTR’s approach of engineering purpose-built investment vehicles tailored to distinct risk appetites and return objectives across equity, credit, yield, and hybrid strategies not only broadens the investor funnel but also lowers the company’s effective cost of capital, enhances balance sheet resilience, and mitigates liquidity risk during potential black swan events. In our view, MSTR’s capital framework represents a structural innovation in public-company finance, positioning the business as a unique, scalable vehicle for BTC exposure while continually unlocking new sources of demand across the global financial ecosystem.”

Quantifying his stance, the analyst rates the stock as Overweight (Buy), with a $213 price target that indicates a potential 34% gain in the year ahead. (To watch El-Assal’s track record, click here)

The 13 recent analyst reviews here split 11 to 2 in favor of Buy over Hold, for a Strong Buy consensus rating. The shares are priced at $158.45, and the $439.36 average price target implies a robust one-year upside of 177%. (See MSTR stock forecast)

data.title: J.P. Morgan Names 2 New Top Stocks to Buy
data.description:
While investors are busy hunting for the next AI superstar, a far less flashy – but hugely consequential – transformation is unfolding along America’s coastlines and inland waterways. Billions of dollars are being poured into rebuilding the nation’s ports, bridges, and flood-control systems, targeting a problem that’s been quietly festering for decades. Compared with other advanced economies, the U.S. has long lagged in maintaining its core infrastructure, and the bill is finally coming due.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, signed in 2021, set in motion roughly $1.2 trillion in federal spending to modernize the country’s physical backbone. A sizable slice of that money is flowing into port expansion, flood-protection projects, especially in the economically vital Mississippi Valley, and broader efforts to make transportation networks more resilient. About $17 billion alone has been earmarked for upgrading ports, waterways, and marine infrastructure nationwide.

Updating America’s aging ports and waterways carries real economic and national security implications, reshaping how goods move across the country and how the U.S. competes globally. And for construction and maritime-infrastructure companies positioned to win federal contracts, it represents a potentially powerful growth tailwind.

Against this backdrop, J.P. Morgan analyst Tomohiko Sano has pinpointed two stocks he believes stand to benefit directly from the port-infrastructure spending wave. We turned to the TipRanks platform to see how other Wall Street analysts view these names, and whether they agree with his call.

Orion Group Holdings (ORN)

We’ll start by looking at a specialty construction company, Orion Group Holdings. Orion has extensive infrastructure projects across the American South and West, with a portfolio that includes more than 5,000 projects in the US, Canada, and the Caribbean. The company’s main operating segments are Marine, Concrete, and Engineering.

Marine is the segment that most concerns us here. Under this rubric, the company provides expertise for a wide range of marine-related infrastructure construction jobs, on and off the water, as well as at and below the surface. Orion’s Marine segment is based in Houston, Texas, and has over a century of experience in the field. The company can build ports and terminals, provide heavy support for heavy marine industry, develop and build transportation infrastructure in marine areas, and even provide environmental restoration services. Orion is also a top-tier dredging service provider for the coastal waterways.

All of that can lean on Orion’s concrete solutions, which have been at the leading edge of the industry since 1980. Orion’s concrete group serves most aspects of the construction sector, and provides concrete and related materials for skyrise projects, data centers, warehouses, industrial facilities – and marine projects. Orion provides the manpower, equipment and technology to get concrete jobs done right.

Over the past two years, Orion’s pipeline has experienced heavy growth, going from $3 billion to $18 billion. The company is benefiting from US Navy contracts, and One Big Beautiful Bill spending, as well as previously approved spending from the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. And earlier this month, Orion was awarded an $86.3 million contract from the US Army Corps of Engineers, for work on the Texas coastline at the mouth of the Colorado River.

JPM’s Tomohiko Sano is impressed by the strong growth in the company’s workload, and writes of Orion, “Marine segment growth is expected from multi-year federal port, waterway, and defense programs, while improved bid discipline targets higher-margin coastal and Navy projects. The Concrete segment should benefit from robust data center and industrial demand. Enhanced backlog quality and execution are supporting margin expansion. With greater visibility into 2026 and operational improvements, we forecast sustained double-digit EPS growth and see meaningful re-rating potential, offering an attractive risk/reward profile.”

Sano puts an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating on the stock, and his $16 price target implies a 32% gain over the next 12 months. (To watch Sano’s track record, click here)

All three of the recent analyst reviews here are positive, for a unanimous Strong Buy consensus rating. The stock has a current trading price of $12.21 and its $14.50 average target price implies a 20% upside for the year ahead. (See ORN stock forecast)

data.title: Bank of America Bets on 2 Little-Known ‘Strong Buy’ AI Chip Stocks
data.description:
The surge in AI investment is rippling through the semiconductor supply chain in ways that go beyond GPUs and CPUs. Building and scaling modern data centers requires a growing amount of specialized connectivity and networking silicon, creating opportunities for companies positioned in the high-speed data movement segment of the market.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

While Nvidia grabs headlines for powering AI brains, a different class of chipmakers is quietly building the nervous system that connects them. As data centers scale to support ever-larger models, the ability to move information quickly and reliably between servers, racks, and processors is becoming just as important as raw computing power.

This shift is reshaping where value is created in the semiconductor ecosystem. Training and running modern AI workloads requires massive volumes of data to be transmitted at extremely high speeds, often across complex networks of hardware. That has turned connectivity, signal integrity, and power efficiency into strategic priorities for hyperscalers racing to expand their AI infrastructure.

The result is a growing focus on the less glamorous layers of the stack – the chips that manage, amplify, and route data rather than process it. These components may not perform calculations, but without them, AI systems cannot scale effectively. As bottlenecks move from compute to communication, spending is beginning to follow, opening the door for suppliers positioned at this critical junction of performance and efficiency.

Bank of America’s 5-star tech expert Vivek Arya has taken this logic to its natural endpoint – he has picked out two lesser-known AI chip stocks to buy at current levels. These are companies connected to the industry – in exactly the supporting roles that are now growing ever more important.

After using TipRanks’ database, we found out that each ticker has also received enough support from other Wall Street analysts to earn a “Strong Buy” consensus rating. Let’s take a closer look.

Credo Technology Group (CRDO)

We’ll start with Credo Technology Group, the California tech company that has both hands fully enmeshed in networking and connectivity. Credo provides solutions and products for the high-speed bandwidth and wired connections that make data infrastructure function – and without which, AI capability would not be possible.

Prominent among Credo’s products are AECs, PCIe retimers, ethernet retimers, and optical solutions. These products provide the direct, high-capacity connections that interlink server racks, connect accelerator clusters, and provide essential GPU-to-GPU connections. Credo assembles these systems based on a high-end core technology, a solid architecture, and a system-level approach that provides a firm foundation.

In practical terms, what this means is that Credo can support AI infrastructure for the largest hyperscalers. The company’s products can meet the high-speed reliability that makes interconnections at the largest scales possible – connecting AI clusters with more than 1 million GPUs while maintaining low latency and high bandwidth, and, at the same time, allowing AI operations to scale up and scale out.

Recent trends – the expansion of AI data centers, the growth of generative and agentic AI – have been good to Credo – and that’s putting it mildly. Even competition from such major names as Marvell and Astera hasn’t been able to derail Credo’s strength. The company reported a huge 272% year-over-year revenue gain in its last quarterly release, which covered fiscal 2Q26. The top line in the quarter reached $268 million and beat the forecast by $33 million. The company’s non-GAAP EPS figure, of 67 cents, was 17 cents per share better than had been anticipated.

Bank of America’s Arya, who is rated by TipRanks among the top 2% of his Wall Street peers, notes both Credo’s status as an early provider of AECs and other high-end data center networking tech, and its ability to withstand competition. He writes, “As a pioneer of the AEC technology, CRDO had enjoyed near 90% share through 2024. Starting in 2025, competition from MRVL/ALAB emerged, with CRDO market share likely declining to the ~70-75% range. Nevertheless, we view CRDO as the technological, reliability, tech transition, and customer engagement leader in AECs with first mover advantage. If CRDO can maintain 50% market share through CY30E, we view AEC sales alone could ~4x to $3.5bn from ~$900bn in CY25E, or $10+ EPS power at current 65% GM, excluding any new meaningful impact from new optical ALCs (active LED cables), PCIe retimers, or omniconnect gearboxes. This is also in-line with: (1) CRDO’s recent $10bn total company TAM outlook by CY30, of which believe CRDO can capture 40-50% overall market share at ~$4-5bn in annual sales, or (2) ~8-10% share assumption of total ~$60bn AI connectivity TAM (5% of $1.2Tn+ AI TAM).”

Quantifying his stance, Arya gives the shares a Buy rating with a $200 price target that implies a 56% upside potential for the coming year. (To watch Arya’s track record, click here)

Shares in CRDO are currently trading for $128.02, and the $224.44 average target price implies a one-year gain of 75%. Overall, the stock holds a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on 12 reviews with a lopsided split of 11 Buys to 1 Hold. (See CRDO stock forecast)

data.title: Goldman Sachs Doubles Down on These 2 Stocks
data.description:
With January nearly in the rearview mirror, Wall Street’s crystal-ball gazers are already sketching out what the rest of 2026 might look like. A few themes are coming into focus: the U.S. economy appears set to keep chugging along, with AI once again acting as a key engine of growth. The Federal Reserve has shifted into easing mode, although just how far rates will fall remains an open question.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The bigger question is whether growth can remain stronger than many expect. According to the team at Goldman Sachs Asset Management, the conditions are already in place for a solid year ahead.

“In the US, activity should stay robust, underpinned by strong AI-related capital expenditure, still-easy financial conditions, and a positive fiscal impulse from the ‘One Big Beautiful Bill,’” the strategists wrote. “Against this macro backdrop, monetary policy is likely to remain a key differentiator across economies… We expect the Fed to cut rates further in 2026, but above-trend growth argues for only limited additional easing. That said, the appointment of a dovish new Fed Chair could point to somewhat more room for cuts, depending on the extent to which the new Chair manages to convince the rest of the FOMC of their policy views.”

With that bullish outlook in mind, Goldman’s stock analysts have recently doubled down on two names. We opened up the TipRanks database to see what’s behind those calls – and what the firm is betting on.

Enphase Energy (ENPH)

We’ll start in the solar industry, where Enphase is a leader in the US – and world – market for residential solar power installations. The company is best known as a supplier of microinverter-based solar-plus-storage systems; microinverters are a vital piece of every solar installation, converting the natural DC output of the photovoltaic panels into AC electric current that can be used in the home.

In addition, Enphase also provides the necessary ancillaries to make a solar installation work for the customer: the connection solutions to link the solar power generation to storage batteries, and an app-based platform for intelligent energy management. The system is designed to bring a high-tech approach to the practical level of residential solar: from power generation to power storage to customer control.

In recent weeks, Enphase has made some announcements that investors should take notice of. In December, the company announced that it had expanded its safe harbor agreement with a solar financing company and expects to generate some $55 million in additional revenue from 4Q25 into 1Q26. And on January 12, the company announced the start of production shipments of its new IQ9 microinverters in the US. The new inverters are manufactured in the US and are Enphase’s first to use gallium nitride (GaN) technology.

All of this leads into Enphase’s financial results. We haven’t yet seen the 4Q25 report, but the 3Q25 results showed a two-year high in quarterly revenue. The $410.4 million top line was up 8% year-over-year and beat the forecast by almost $41 million. Enphase realized a non-GAAP earnings figure of 90 cents per share, 24 cents per share better than had been previously expected.

Laying out the Goldman Sachs view here, analyst Brian Lee explains why this solar company is in a good place to keep running forward. He writes, “We see ENPH poised to enter 2026 with some momentum, including lean channel inventories that provide a cleaner set up heading into 2026. At the same time, ENPH’s valuation remains at or near all-time lows, and risk-reward appears to be more favorable than it has been in some time, especially considering investor sentiment remains sharply skewed negative (based on elevated short interest levels) despite what we see as potential for a near-term beat vs. consensus expectations.”

Accordingly, Lee upgraded the rating on ENPH to a Buy (from a Neutral) and sets a $45 price target that implies an upside of 14% for the next 12 months. (To watch Lee’s track record, click here)

That view is decidedly more bullish than the consensus on Enphase, which gets a Hold from Wall Street – based on 22 reviews that include 7 Buys, 10 Holds, and 5 Sells. The shares are priced at $39.46, and their $39.05 average price target indicates they are currently fully valued. (See ENPH stock forecast)
